item.title: Stop pointing your tests at a real Auth0 tenant
item.description:
Your service talks to Auth0. Your tests, therefore, also talk to Auth0.

That’s the moment things start to rot. The test suite needs a tenant. The tenant has rate limits. Two engineers run the suite at once and step on each other’s users. CI flakes because a token took 900ms to mint instead of 200ms. Someone hard-codes a fake JWT to skip the network, and now your signature-verification path is the one thing your tests never actually exercise.

item.title: Most engineering initiatives don't fail at execution
item.description:
Every initiative I’ve watched die looked the same in the post-mortem.

Not “we chose the wrong stack.” Not “the team underperformed.” It was always some version of: we didn’t really understand what we were building until halfway through.

The interesting part is that this is fixable. It just requires the one step most engineers skip, because it doesn’t feel like work.

The work that doesn’t look like work

Senior engineers spend more time on planning than juniors realize. That’s not a slogan, it’s a bias I’ve watched play out in every team I’ve worked with: the most reliable shippers are the ones who appear to be doing nothing for the first week of a project. They’re not. They’re doing the part that determines whether the next three months go well.

item.title: OpenFGA: the authorization layer your code keeps trying to reinvent
item.description:
Open any backend that’s three years old. Find the if user.role == "admin" in your handlers. Now find the second copy three files away, slightly different. The third inside a service method. The fourth, in a database query.

You’re not maintaining authorization logic. You’re scattering it.

This is the problem OpenFGA was built to solve. It’s the open-source implementation of Google’s Zanzibar paper, the system that powers Google Drive’s sharing model. Instead of asking “what role does this user have?”, you ask “is there a relationship between this user and this object that lets them do this?”.

item.title: An encrypted Arch Linux install you can actually maintain
item.description:
Most Arch install guides hand you a working system. They don’t hand you one you can fix at 3am when the bootloader misbehaves.

This is the install I run on my own machines. Full disk encryption with LUKS, dynamic volumes via LVM, copy-pasteable commands, and just enough explanation that you’ll know what to undo when something inevitably goes sideways.

I’ve been running Arch for years. The thing that keeps me here isn’t speed, isn’t the AUR, isn’t rolling releases. It’s that every part of the system is something I picked, which means every part is something I can debug later.

item.title: About me
item.description:
~/whoami

STATUS online
LOCATION Valencia, ES (39.47°N · 0.38°W)
STACK go · terraform · k8s · openfga · postgres
OFFLINE food · travel · repeat

Senior software engineer based in Spain.

Day to day: Go, Terraform, fine-grained authorization, and the backend infrastructure underneath. Usually on problems where they pull on each other. I lean broad rather than deep, which is useful when the problem doesn’t fit a single specialty. Most of what I build is in service of developer experience: the small frictions that compound into real costs when you ignore them. Always up for a hard problem, always looking to make the next one less painful.
